name: ARIA state or property has valid value
description: This rule checks that each ARIA state or property has a valid value type.
applicability_text: This rule applies to any [WAI-ARIA state or property][] that has a non-empty ("") [attribute value][], and that is specified on an [HTML or SVG element][].
expectations:
Each test target has an [attribute value][] that is valid according to its [WAI-ARIA value type][value type].

**Exception**: For value types `ID Reference` and `ID Reference List` no ID referenced elements are required.

examples.name: Passed Example 1
examples.description: The `aria-label` [attribute value][] of `Family name` is allowed for the `string` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 2
examples.description: The `aria-required` [attribute value][] of `true` is allowed for the `true/false` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 3
examples.description: The `aria-expanded` [attribute value][] of `undefined` is allowed for the `true/false/undefined` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 4
examples.description: The `aria-pressed` [attribute value][] of `mixed` is allowed for the `tristate` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 5
examples.description: The `aria-errormessage` [attribute value][] is an `ID Reference` [value type][]. The presence of an element with a matching ID is not required by this rule.

examples.name: Passed Example 6
examples.description: The `aria-owns` [attribute value][] is a valid `ID Reference List` [value type][]. Both tokens reference elements that exist in the same [DOM tree][].
examples.code:
Shopping list

Apples
Bananas

examples.name: Passed Example 7
examples.description: The `aria-rowindex` [attribute value][] of 2 is a valid `integer` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 8
examples.description: The `aria-valuemin`, `aria-valuemax` and `aria-valuenow` [attribute values][attribute value] are valid for a `number` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 9
examples.description: The `aria-current` [attribute value][] of `page` is a valid `token` [value type][].

examples.name: Passed Example 10
examples.description: The `aria-relevant` [attribute value][] has a `text` and `removals` tokens. Both are valid tokens for a `aria-relevant` `token list` [value type][].

examples.name: Failed Example 1
examples.description: The `aria-required` [attribute value][] of `undefined` is not valid for a `true/false` [value type][].

examples.name: Failed Example 2
examples.description: The `aria-expanded` [attribute value][] of `collapsed` is not valid for a `true/false/undefined` [value type][].

examples.name: Failed Example 3
examples.description: The `aria-pressed` [attribute value][] of `horizontal` is not valid for a `tristate` [value type][].

examples.name: Failed Example 4
examples.description: The `aria-rowindex` [attribute value][] of `2.5` is not valid for an `integer` [value type][] because it is a decimal number.

examples.name: Failed Example 5
examples.description: The `aria-valuemin`, `aria-valuemax` and `aria-valuenow` [attribute values][attribute value] are `strings`. These should all be of the `number` [value type][] instead.

examples.name: Failed Example 6
examples.description: The `aria-live` [attribute value][] of `page` is not a valid `token`, because `page` is not a token for `aria-live`.

examples.name: Failed Example 7
examples.description: The `aria-relevant` [attribute value][] has the two tokens `text` and `always`. The `always` token is not valid for the `aria-relevant` `token list`. In order to be a valid value, all tokens must be valid.

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 1
examples.description: Element does not have any ARIA states or properties

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 2
examples.description: Element has ARIA role, but no ARIA states or properties

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 3
examples.description: The `aria-live` attribute does not have a value.

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 4
examples.description: The `aria-hidden` attribute is not on an [HTML or SVG element][].

background:
Using invalid ARIA attribute values is often the result of a typo or other developer error. These attributes are then either ignored, or a default value is assumed by browsers and assistive technologies. This often means that a state or property which should exist is missing or has an unexpected value. If the default value for invalid attribute values happens to match the author's intention for the value, there will not be an accessibility issue.

This rule does not require the target of an `ID Reference` to exist. This is because referencing an element that does not exist, and not having the reference at all has the same end result. A common use case for using `ID Reference` for a non-existing ID is to use a static `aria-errormessage` on an `input` element, and to only insert the element with the error message if there is an actual error. There are some cases in which ID references are required. These are tested in a separate rule.
accessibility_support: Some user agents treat the value of `aria-*` attribute as case-sensitive (even when these are not ID) while some treat them as case-insensitive.
